does: This paper trains language models to serve as activation explainers—systems that interpret their own internal neural activations by answering natural language questions about them. The authors create a training setup where models learn to describe what different activation patterns represent, then evaluate how well these trained explainers generalize to diverse settings including detecting alignment shifts caused by fine-tuning. The core contribution is demonstrating that LLMs can be bootstrapped into this self-interpretability role and maintaining accuracy across distribution shifts where typical mechanistic interpretability tools might fail.
connects: This work builds on the growing field of mechanistic interpretability, which seeks to understand neural networks by identifying and analyzing individual neuron or activation patterns' functions. It extends prior work on neural network interpretability by leveraging language models' natural language capabilities—rather than using hand-crafted probes or statistical methods to characterize activations, the paper uses the models themselves as interpreters. This connects to recent efforts in explainability that treat language as a medium for making neural systems more transparent, building on techniques that use language models for explanation generation and steering alignment.
enables: This approach opens pathways for practical interpretability tools that don't require specialized technical knowledge to deploy. By having models explain their own activations in natural language, the work could enable non-experts to audit model behavior and detect unintended changes from fine-tuning or other modifications. Future work could extend activation oracles to real-time monitoring of model behavior, certification of alignment properties, or integration into training loops for automated detection of specification gaming or value drift. The framework also suggests that language models could serve as general-purpose interpreters for other neural systems.
